content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Nice narrative and clash :-)

Title
* Will there be a LVL 2, LVL 3, etc?

Input
* Next n lines: Please specify "x and y" in the description, since the variable names a and b don't clearly indicate which values they represent.

Constraints
* Please specify constraints for all variables, and include both the lower and upper limits, e.g.
-10000 ≤ [[x]], [[y]], [[a]], [[b]] ≤ 10000
1 ≤ r ≤ 10000
1 ≤ n ≤ 50

Stub generator
* Please add a write statement, e.g. write answer.

Cases
* Each test and its validator should form a pair and cover the same scenario with slightly different inputs (of similar magnitude) and outputs. Please adjust the cases accordingly.

* Please use a balanced range of random values as inputs.

Thanks.

Thank you so much for your feedback, as this is my first time making a contribution! As for the title, I actually do plan on making a LVL 2 or maybe even a LVL 3 version of the concept of TD Targeting, requiring more computations as difficulty increases. So, I kind of want to keep LVL 1 in the title if that's okay with you
OMG! Thanks so much for approving! This is my first contribution, so I'm glad that you liked it. Just wondering, how would you rank the difficulty?

Thank you for taking the time to reply and update your contribution.

Regarding the naming, while the following situations may not necessarily apply in your case, but they do often occur:

* Not all parts of a contribution get approved.
* An author stops after writing only one part.
* Limited progression of difficulty (since a Clash of Code should be kept easy).

That said, part naming is less of an issue in Clash of Code, since contributions are usually not publicly visible after approval. Changes to the name later generally don't create problems like they might with puzzle counterparts. I do hope you continue working on the next parts and get them approved so the part names remain consistent and logical!

As for the updated contribution:

Cases
* The range of input values used in the tests is still more limited than in the corresponding validators:
  • Tests: Almost all input values are single-digit or double-digit integers.
  • Validators: Input values cover a broader, more balanced range, particularly in the positive range.
  • Overall: Only one input value is less than -100.
  
  As mentioned before, inputs for a pair of cases should be of similar magnitude. Ideally, the overall test set should include a balanced range of random values (between -10,000 and 10,000) Please revise the cases to reflect this.

Thanks again.

I ended up just deciding to make the title simpler to avoid confusion.

Also, I changed the test cases and validators so that their overall magnitudes were similar. Also added more variety of negative numbers, but please don't make me fix the last validator by adding negatives to the y-coordinates because I would have a HUGE headache from doing so, and I'm gonna crash out if I have to deal with that. Sorry that I'm too lazy, but I do have a life you know

Thanks for the update. The revised cases look much better now.

Just to clarify something important: there's absolutely no obligation to finish or rush a contribution. If you don't currently have the time or patience to make a small adjustment, that's completely fine; you can simply leave the contribution in draft/WIP and come back to it whenever you like. There's no deadline or pressure to push it through review.

(Also… are you implying the reviewer doesn't have a life? 😄)

One practical tip that may help in the future: rather than hand-crafting cases, it's often much easier to write a small random case generator. This makes it trivial to produce balanced datasets and avoids the kind of tedious adjustments you mentioned. Case creation becomes much faster and far less painful.
